definition: Lasting for a very short time; transitory.
example: The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms draws crowds each spring, even though they fade within days.
memory_tip: Think of an ephemeral experience — like a snap that disappears!
wrong_definitions: Occurring repeatedly and frequently
Extremely large or vast in scope
Deeply rooted and permanent

definition: A person who flatters powerful people to gain personal advantage.
example: The CEO was surrounded by sycophants who praised every decision, no matter how flawed.
memory_tip: A sycophant is like a 'sick fan' — obsessively praising someone!
wrong_definitions: A fierce critic who never holds back opinions
Someone who works in complete silence
A person known for their generosity

definition: Present, appearing, or found everywhere at the same time.
example: Smartphones have become so ubiquitous that it's rare to see someone without one.
memory_tip: Ubiquitous = 'you be everywhere!' — it's everywhere you look!
wrong_definitions: Extremely rare and hard to find
Related to ancient cultures and traditions
Occurring only during specific seasons

definition: Mental calmness and composure, especially in difficult situations.
example: She faced the devastating news with remarkable equanimity, staying focused and composed.
memory_tip: Equanimity = 'equal' + 'animity' — keeping your spirit equal/balanced!
wrong_definitions: Extreme anger and loss of self-control
A tendency to overthink every problem
The feeling of overwhelming excitement

definition: Having a ready insight into things; shrewd and discerning.
example: The perspicacious investor spotted the startup's potential before anyone else did.
memory_tip: Per-SPIC-acious — you can SPY through things with sharp insight!
wrong_definitions: Unable to focus or concentrate on tasks
Extremely talkative and outgoing
Prone to making poor decisions

definition: Stubbornly resistant to authority or control; uncooperative.
example: The recalcitrant student refused to follow classroom rules despite repeated warnings.
memory_tip: Recalcitrant — like a stubborn CALF that won't be herded!
wrong_definitions: Enthusiastically cooperative and eager to help
Prone to excessive worry and anxiety
Having an excellent memory for details

definition: Clear, logical, and convincing in argument.
example: The lawyer presented a cogent argument that swayed the entire jury.
memory_tip: COGent — like a cog that fits perfectly and drives the machine forward!
wrong_definitions: Vague and difficult to understand
Overly emotional and illogical
Long-winded and repetitive

root_meaning: In the manner of Spartans (known for brief speech)
definition: Using very few words; brief and concise in speech.
example: His laconic reply of 'Fine' revealed nothing about how he truly felt.
memory_tip: Laconic — LAC of words! Lacking extra words!
wrong_definitions: Talkative and prone to lengthy speeches
Extremely emotional when speaking
Using complex vocabulary constantly

definition: Very generous or forgiving, especially toward a rival or less powerful person.
example: Despite winning by a huge margin, she was magnanimous and praised her competitors.
memory_tip: MAGN-animous — MAGNIFICENT soul! Great and giving!
wrong_definitions: Extremely petty and resentful of others
Reluctant to share resources with anyone
Overly competitive and self-centered

definition: Deceitful and untrustworthy; guilty of betrayal.
example: The perfidious advisor secretly passed secrets to the enemy while pretending loyalty.
memory_tip: PERFIDIOUS — PERFIDY is betrayal, so perfidious = treacherous!
wrong_definitions: Completely honest and transparent
Extremely loyal even under pressure
Shy and reluctant to speak

definition: Fearless and adventurous; bold and daring.
example: The intrepid explorer crossed the frozen tundra despite extreme and dangerous conditions.
memory_tip: IN-TREPID — not trepidatious (fearful)! Fearless!
wrong_definitions: Extremely cautious and risk-averse
Timid and easily frightened
Overly careful and hesitant

definition: Fundamentally different in kind; impossible to compare directly.
example: The team brought together people with disparate backgrounds to solve the problem creatively.
memory_tip: DISPAR-ate — DISPARate things don't share a pair of qualities!
wrong_definitions: Extremely similar and nearly identical
Gradually becoming the same over time
Related by a common ancestor

definition: Showing a casual lack of concern; blithely indifferent.
example: He strolled into the meeting ten minutes late with an insouciant shrug.
memory_tip: IN-SOUCIANT — IN without a SOUS (care/cent) to your name about it!
wrong_definitions: Extremely anxious and worried about everything
Deeply committed and passionately concerned
Intensely focused and detail-oriented

definition: Giving or indicating a good chance of success; favorable.
example: The clear skies created propitious conditions for the outdoor graduation ceremony.
memory_tip: PRO-pitious — PRO means for! Propitious is FOR you, favorable!
wrong_definitions: Indicating a very high chance of failure
Completely neutral with no advantages
Creating dangerous and unstable conditions

definition: Open to more than one interpretation; deliberately vague or ambiguous.
example: The politician's equivocal response left reporters unsure whether he supported the bill.
memory_tip: EQUI-vocal — EQUAL voices pointing in two directions at once!
wrong_definitions: Extremely direct and unambiguous
Using only one specific meaning
Communicating with perfect clarity

definition: To make something bad or unsatisfactory better; to improve.
example: The new policies were designed to ameliorate the living conditions in struggling neighborhoods.
memory_tip: AMELIO-rate — rate things as A+ = MELIORATE = make better!
wrong_definitions: To make something significantly worse
To keep things exactly the same
To destroy completely without trace

definition: Having a harmful effect, especially in a gradual or subtle way.
example: The pernicious influence of misinformation slowly eroded public trust in science.
memory_tip: PER-NICIOUS — nasty to the NINES, per(fectly) harmful!
wrong_definitions: Extremely beneficial and health-promoting
Completely harmless and safe
Temporarily unpleasant but ultimately helpful

definition: To make something obscure, unclear, or confusing; to muddle.
example: The dense legal language seemed designed to obfuscate rather than clarify the terms.
memory_tip: OBF-USCATE — to put a USCATe (haze) OVER something, obscuring it!
wrong_definitions: To explain clearly and in great detail
To illuminate and make transparent
To simplify a complex situation

definition: Tending to talk a great deal; very talkative.
example: The loquacious tour guide entertained the group with endless stories and anecdotes.
memory_tip: LOQUA-cious — LOQUACIOUSly talking, LOQUAT sounds like 'lots of words'!
wrong_definitions: Silent and reluctant to communicate
Speaking in a very quiet, barely audible voice
Communicating only through writing

definition: Eager or quick to argue or fight; aggressively defiant.
example: The truculent customer argued with every staff member and refused all solutions.
memory_tip: TRUCULENT — like a truck that BULLS through everything aggressively!
wrong_definitions: Calm, peaceful, and easy to work with
Shy and reluctant to speak up
Overly eager to please and accommodate

definition: Holding firmly to a purpose or goal; persistent and determined.
example: Despite repeated failures, the tenacious scientist continued her research for decades.
memory_tip: TENA-cious — TENAcity means holding on TIGHT, like a TENAcle!
wrong_definitions: Quick to give up when faced with difficulty
Inconsistent and easily distracted
Overly aggressive and combative

definition: Demonstrating aggression and willingness to fight; warlike.
example: The bellicose nation threatened its neighbors and refused all diplomatic solutions.
memory_tip: BELLICOSE — BELLI-COSE sounds like 'belly-chose war'! Warlike!
wrong_definitions: Peaceful and strongly opposed to conflict
Indifferent to conflict or politics
Cooperative and eager to negotiate

definition: Given to sudden and unaccountable changes of mood or behavior; unpredictable.
example: The capricious weather shifted from sunshine to hail within minutes.
memory_tip: CAPRICIOUS — a goat (CAPRI) jumps randomly! Capriciously unpredictable!
wrong_definitions: Extremely consistent and highly predictable
Slow to change and resistant to new ideas
Carefully planned and methodical

definition: Angry and bitter, especially in speech or manner.
example: The divorce proceedings became acrimonious, with both parties hurling accusations.
memory_tip: ACRI-monious — ACRID (harsh smell) + MONI = sharp, bitter words!
wrong_definitions: Warm, friendly, and full of goodwill
Completely neutral and detached
Enthusiastic and celebratory

definition: To make an unpleasant feeling less intense; to relieve or soothe.
example: She tried to assuage her friend's fears with reassuring words and a warm hug.
memory_tip: ASSUAGE — AS SWEET as possible to SOOTHE! AS-SUAGE your pain away!
wrong_definitions: To intensify and worsen a bad feeling
To ignore a problem completely
To create new problems and tensions

definition: Wary and unwilling to take risks; carefully considering all circumstances.
example: The circumspect investor researched every detail before committing to the venture.
memory_tip: CIRCUM-SPECT — to inspect all AROUND before deciding! Look before you leap!
wrong_definitions: Reckless and prone to impulsive decisions
Overly trusting without any hesitation
Completely indifferent to consequences

definition: Intended to teach, especially having moral instruction as an ulterior motive.
example: The didactic novel about the dangers of greed felt more like a lecture than a story.
memory_tip: DIDAC-tic — DIDACTic = TAUGHT (like a dictation)! Intended to teach!
wrong_definitions: Purely entertaining without any educational value
Confusing and impossible to understand
Emotionally engaging without any lesson

definition: Outstandingly bad; shockingly noticeable in a negative way.
example: The judge called the lawyer's behavior an egregious violation of courtroom ethics.
memory_tip: E-GREE-gious — so bad it makes you want to GREE-n with disgust!
wrong_definitions: Exceptionally good and praiseworthy
Completely average and unremarkable
Slightly better than normal

definition: Very attentive to detail and accuracy; difficult to please; very exacting.
example: The fastidious chef rejected any dish that didn't meet his exacting standards.
memory_tip: FASTID-ious — being FUSSY and TIDY = FASTIDIOUS about every detail!
wrong_definitions: Careless and indifferent to quality
Quick to settle for less than perfect
Extremely lazy and unmotivated

definition: Excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.
example: The garrulous neighbor recounted every detail of her vacation for two solid hours.
memory_tip: GARRU-lous — like a parrot that GARS (talks) incessantly! Nonstop chatter!
wrong_definitions: Extremely quiet and reserved
Communicating only through actions
Speaking only when absolutely necessary

definition: Acting or done quickly without thought or care; impulsive.
example: His impetuous decision to quit his job without a backup plan shocked his family.
memory_tip: IMPETUOUS — IMPETU-ous decisions have lots of IMPETUS (force) but no planning!
wrong_definitions: Extremely careful and thoughtful before acting
Slow and methodical in decision-making
Hesitant and prone to procrastination

definition: Not telling the truth; lying; deceitful.
example: The mendacious politician had a long record of making false promises.
memory_tip: MENDA-cious — MENDA (mend) + CIOUS — you MEND lies to make them believable!
wrong_definitions: Always honest and transparent
Reluctant to share any information
Known for careful, thorough research

definition: Wicked or criminal in a flagrant way.
example: The villain's nefarious scheme involved fraud, blackmail, and theft.
memory_tip: NEFAR-ious — 'nefarious' NEFAR = sounds like 'no fair!' Wicked and unfair!
wrong_definitions: Extremely kind and charitable
Completely lawful and by-the-book
Misguided but well-intentioned

definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or course of action.
example: The obdurate manager refused to hear any suggestions for improvement from her team.
memory_tip: OB-DURATE — OBstinately DURABLE in your position! Hard as a rock!
wrong_definitions: Open-minded and willing to change
Easily persuaded by new evidence
Eager to consider alternative viewpoints

definition: Characterized by vulgar or pretentious display; designed to impress.
example: The ostentatious mansion had gold-plated faucets and a private bowling alley.
memory_tip: OSTENTA-tious — OSTEN = to show off, TATIOU = lavishly! Showing everything off!
wrong_definitions: Modest and understated in presentation
Simple and functional without decoration
Hidden away and deliberately secretive

definition: Not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily; reserved; quiet.
example: Although brilliant, she was reticent in class and rarely volunteered answers.
memory_tip: RETI-CENT — RETI as in retired/holding back + CENT = holding back your two cents!
wrong_definitions: Extremely talkative and open about feelings
Eager to share opinions at every opportunity
Known for being outgoing and expressive

definition: Grimly mocking or cynical; disdainfully humorous.
example: His sardonic comment about their 'brilliant' plan made everyone uncomfortable.
memory_tip: SAR-DONIC — SARcastic + ironic = SARDONIC! Bitter, mocking humor!
wrong_definitions: Warm and genuinely encouraging
Innocent and straightforward humor
Enthusiastic and full of sincerity

definition: Behaving or looking as though one is superior to others; condescending.
example: The supercilious waiter made guests feel unwelcome with his disdainful looks.
memory_tip: SUPER-cilious — 'SUPER' is above everyone, CILIOU = looking down with raised brows!
wrong_definitions: Extremely humble and self-deprecating
Kind and respectful to everyone equally
Shy and uncomfortable around others

definition: Having or showing a lack of thought or intelligence; empty.
example: The reality show was filled with vacuous celebrities who had nothing meaningful to say.
memory_tip: VACU-ous — like a VACUUM — empty inside! Vacuous means nothing inside!
wrong_definitions: Filled with deep wisdom and insight
Densely packed with important ideas
Known for profound and original thinking

definition: Extremely harmful or poisonous; bitterly hostile.
example: The virulent disease spread rapidly, overwhelming hospitals across the region.
memory_tip: VIRU-lent — VIRUS + LENT = filled with (virus) poison! Deadly and hostile!
wrong_definitions: Completely harmless and easily treated
Beneficial to the immune system
Spreading goodwill and positive energy

definition: To lower someone in rank, status, or dignity; to humiliate.
example: The dictator used public humiliations to abase political opponents and break their spirit.
memory_tip: A-BASE — to bring someone down to their BASE level, stripping their dignity!
wrong_definitions: To elevate someone to a higher position
To praise someone publicly and sincerely
To grant someone additional authority

definition: To become less intense, severe, or widespread; to diminish.
example: After hours of heavy rain, the storm finally began to abate, and the sun emerged.
memory_tip: ABATE — like a BAIT that FADES. The storm takes the bait and fades away!
wrong_definitions: To grow stronger and more intense over time
To remain perfectly constant without change
To spread rapidly to new areas

definition: To regard with disgust and hatred; to detest intensely.
example: She abhorred dishonesty and refused to work with anyone who had deceived her.
memory_tip: AB-HOR — ABsolutely HORrified! To abhor is to feel total horror toward something!
wrong_definitions: To admire deeply and with great respect
To feel neutral or indifferent toward
To tolerate despite personal reservations

definition: Brisk and cheerful readiness; eager willingness to do something.
example: The volunteers tackled the cleanup project with remarkable alacrity, finishing hours ahead of schedule.
memory_tip: ALAC-RITY — ALERT + CITY = an ALERT, quick-moving energy! Ready to go!
wrong_definitions: Extreme reluctance and unwillingness to act
A slow and cautious approach to tasks
Deep fatigue and lack of motivation

root_meaning: To lay down or put to rest
definition: To diminish or put at rest (fear, suspicion, or worry); to relieve.
example: The doctor's calm explanation helped allay the patient's fears about the surgery.
memory_tip: ALLAY — to LAY fears to rest, making them go AWAY. ALL-AY your worries!
wrong_definitions: To intensify a fear or concern
To completely ignore a problem
To create new sources of worry

root: ambi (Latin) = both ways + agere = to drive
definition: Open to more than one interpretation; not having a clear meaning.
example: The contract contained ambiguous wording that each side interpreted differently in court.
memory_tip: AMBI-guous — AMBIdextrous uses BOTH hands; ambiguous goes BOTH ways in meaning!
wrong_definitions: Having only one clear and obvious meaning
Expressed with perfect precision and clarity
Containing no room for misunderstanding

root: ambi (Latin) = both + valere = to be strong
definition: Having mixed or contradictory feelings about something or someone.
example: She felt ambivalent about moving abroad — excited for adventure but sad to leave family.
memory_tip: AMBI-VALENT — valuing BOTH sides equally! Pulled in two directions at once!
wrong_definitions: Completely certain and confident in one's feelings
Feeling nothing at all about a situation
Strongly committed to a single viewpoint

definition: A deep-seated feeling of dislike; strong aversion.
example: His antipathy toward loud music made every concert feel like an ordeal.
memory_tip: ANTI-PATHY — ANTI (against) + PATH = a feeling that blocks your PATH to liking something!
wrong_definitions: A powerful feeling of attraction and admiration
Complete emotional indifference
A mild and temporary discomfort

definition: Great enthusiasm or passion; intense emotion.
example: The young activist spoke with such ardor that the crowd was moved to join the cause.
memory_tip: ARDOR — ARDently burning! The FIRE of passion, like an ARDent flame!
wrong_definitions: A cool and detached attitude toward a goal
Complete lack of interest or motivation
Mild, passing curiosity about a subject

definition: Characterized by severe self-discipline; abstaining from pleasure and comfort.
example: The ascetic monk owned nothing but a robe and slept on a bare stone floor.
memory_tip: ASCETIC — ACID + STRICT = a strictly acid-test lifestyle, stripped of all luxuries!
wrong_definitions: Devoted to luxury and extravagant living
Seeking pleasure and comfort above all else
Known for lavish spending and indulgence

definition: Showing great care, diligence, and perseverance; hardworking.
example: The assiduous student reviewed her notes every night and aced every exam.
memory_tip: ASSI-DUOUS — ASSIduous people ASSIST themselves by working DUOUSLY (doubly hard)!
wrong_definitions: Lazy and prone to avoiding effort
Working only when closely supervised
Quick to abandon tasks when they become difficult

definition: Having or showing an ability to assess situations accurately; shrewd.
example: The astute businesswoman spotted the market opportunity months before her competitors.
memory_tip: AS-TUTE — a CUTE sharp mind! AS TUTE as a tutor — clever and perceptive!
wrong_definitions: Unable to read people or situations accurately
Easily fooled and taken advantage of
Slow to understand complex situations

definition: Showing a willingness to take bold, surprisingly daring risks.
example: The audacious heist involved disguises, forged documents, and a helicopter escape.
memory_tip: AUDA-cious — AUDIO turned up to max! Boldly LOUD and daring in every action!
wrong_definitions: Timid and easily intimidated by challenges
Cautious and unwilling to take any risks
Obedient and unlikely to break the rules

definition: Giving a good indication of future success; favorable; promising.
example: Winning the first game of the season was an auspicious start for the new coach.
memory_tip: AUSPI-cious — SUSPICIOUS of good luck? AUSPICIOUS means the omens are FOR you!
wrong_definitions: Indicating a very likely failure ahead
Showing warning signs of coming trouble
Completely neutral with no implications for success

definition: Extreme greed for wealth or material gain; insatiable desire for riches.
example: His avarice drove him to steal from his own family to fund his lavish lifestyle.
memory_tip: AVAR-ICE — HAVE-RICE: always wanting to HAVE more RICE (wealth)! Greedy!
wrong_definitions: A deep generosity and desire to give wealth away
Complete indifference to money and possessions
A preference for simple and humble living

definition: High-sounding but having little real meaning; inflated and pompous in speech.
example: The politician's bombastic speech was full of grand promises but offered no real plan.
memory_tip: BOMB-astic — it BOMBS with big BLASTS of hot air! All explosion, no substance!
wrong_definitions: Simple, direct, and to the point
Carefully reasoned and precise in language
Quiet and understated in delivery

definition: The quality of being open and honest; frankness; sincerity.
example: We appreciated her candor when she told us exactly what went wrong in the project.
memory_tip: CAN-DOR — CAN you be honest? CAN't hide the truth with CANDOR — it's wide open!
wrong_definitions: A habit of hiding one's true thoughts
The tendency to exaggerate and embellish
Deliberate vagueness to avoid controversy

definition: Sarcastic in a scathing and biting way; able to burn or corrode.
example: Her caustic wit could reduce even the most confident person to silence.
memory_tip: CAUSTIC — CAUST like COMBUSTION! Burns through things — even feelings!
wrong_definitions: Gentle and carefully considerate of others' feelings
Warm, encouraging, and supportive in tone
Neutral and completely unemotional

definition: Mercy and leniency shown toward an offender or enemy; mildness.
example: The governor showed clemency by pardoning the prisoner who had reformed his life.
memory_tip: CLEMENT weather is MILD! CLEMENCY is mild, merciful treatment — like calm weather!
wrong_definitions: Harsh and severe punishment without mercy
The strict enforcement of rules with no exceptions
Indifference to the suffering of others

definition: Extremely complex and difficult to follow; intricately twisted.
example: The convoluted tax code confused even experienced accountants.
memory_tip: CON-VOLUTED — COILED and VOLUMINOUS, twisted into a knot! Complex and tangled!
wrong_definitions: Remarkably simple and easy to understand
Perfectly clear and logically organized
Brief and to the point

definition: Abundant in supply or quantity; in large amounts.
example: She took copious notes during the lecture, filling an entire notebook.
memory_tip: COPI-ous — COPY after COPY after COPY! Lots and lots of everything!
wrong_definitions: Extremely scarce and difficult to find
Barely sufficient to meet basic needs
Present in only tiny, insignificant amounts

root: danter (Old French) = to tame or subdue
definition: Seeming difficult to deal with in anticipation; intimidating.
example: Climbing Everest seemed a daunting challenge, but she trained for two years and succeeded.
memory_tip: DAUNT-ing — a DAUNT is a dare that's DAUNTING — makes you hesitate with fear!
wrong_definitions: Remarkably simple and easily achievable
Encouraging and full of positive energy
Completely familiar and comfortable

definition: A sudden, complete disaster or failure; a chaotic collapse.
example: The product launch was a debacle — the website crashed and orders were lost.
memory_tip: DE-BACLE — the BACLE (bar) breaks and everything FALLS apart! Total chaos!
wrong_definitions: A carefully planned and flawlessly executed event
A slow, gradual improvement over time
A minor setback that is easily fixed

definition: Causing harm or damage; injurious to health or well-being.
example: Smoking has well-documented deleterious effects on the lungs and cardiovascular system.
memory_tip: DELETE-rious — DELETE = to erase! Deleterious DELETES your health — it's harmful!
wrong_definitions: Remarkably beneficial to one's health
Completely harmless and neutral in effect
Gradually improving conditions over time

definition: Modest or shy because of a lack of self-confidence; reserved.
example: Despite her talent, she was too diffident to audition for the lead role.
memory_tip: DIFFI-DENT — DIFFICULT + CONFIDENT = not confident at all! Shy and modest!
wrong_definitions: Brash and overconfident in every situation
Openly proud of one's abilities and achievements
Eager to show off skills in front of others

root_meaning: Having a double or two-faced nature
definition: Deceitfulness; saying one thing and meaning or doing another.
example: His duplicity was revealed when both parties discovered he had been negotiating against them.
memory_tip: DUPLI-CITY — DUPLICATE face! Two-faced, double-dealing in the CITY of lies!
wrong_definitions: Complete transparency and total honesty
Consistency between one's words and actions
A tendency to say what one means directly

definition: Cheerful and full of energy; overflowing with enthusiasm.
example: Her ebullient personality lit up every room she walked into.
memory_tip: E-BULL-ient — a BULL full of energy, BUBBLING over with excitement! Energetic!
wrong_definitions: Quiet, withdrawn, and emotionally flat
Deeply pessimistic and hard to cheer up
Reserved and uncomfortable in social settings

definition: Having or showing great knowledge or learning; scholarly.
example: The erudite professor could quote literature, science, and philosophy from memory.
memory_tip: ERU-DITE — like a RUDE-KNIGHT of knowledge, armed with DITE (learning)!
wrong_definitions: Poorly educated and lacking basic knowledge
Knowledgeable only in one narrow area
Unwilling to learn or accept new ideas

root: esōterikos (Greek) = belonging to an inner circle
root_meaning: Known only by a chosen few
definition: Intended for or understood by only a small number of people with special knowledge.
example: The lecture on quantum topology was so esoteric that few students could follow along.
memory_tip: ESO-TERIC — ESO = inner! Like an INNER circle of knowledge, SECRET to outsiders!
wrong_definitions: Commonly known and understood by nearly everyone
Simple enough for young children to grasp
Widely taught in every school curriculum

definition: Having or displaying a passionate intensity; intensely enthusiastic.
example: The fervent crowd chanted nonstop for two hours at the championship rally.
memory_tip: FERVENT — FERVENT-ly boiling! Like FEVER + ENT — burning with intense passion!
wrong_definitions: Calm and emotionally detached
Mildly interested but not enthusiastic
Reluctant and unenthusiastic about a cause

definition: Attempting to avoid notice or attention; secretive and sly.
example: He cast a furtive glance over his shoulder before slipping the note under the door.
memory_tip: FURTIVE — like a THIEF in FUR sneaking around! FURtive = sneaky and stealthy!
wrong_definitions: Openly confident and without any attempt to hide
Loud and attention-seeking in behavior
Straightforward and completely transparent

root_meaning: Fond of being in a flock
definition: Fond of company; sociable; enjoying the company of others.
example: The gregarious student made friends on her first day at every new school she attended.
memory_tip: GREG-arious — like GREG, who's always surrounded by a GROUP! Social butterfly!
wrong_definitions: Reclusive and actively avoiding social interaction
Hostile and unfriendly toward strangers
Comfortable only in one-on-one settings

root: Hackney (a London district) = overused hired horses
definition: Lacking originality or freshness; made tedious by overuse; clichéd.
example: The screenplay was full of hackneyed phrases and predictable plot twists.
memory_tip: HACK-NEYED — a HACK (bad writer) uses tired, worn NEYED (knackered) old phrases!
wrong_definitions: Strikingly original and brilliantly creative
Surprising and unlike anything seen before
Fresh and newly coined for the first time

definition: Proceeding in a gradual, subtle way but with harmful effects; treacherous.
example: The insidious spread of misinformation was hard to combat because it looked like fact.
memory_tip: IN-SIDIOUS — the danger HIDES INSIDE, like a trap waiting to spring!
wrong_definitions: Obvious and openly destructive
Completely harmless and easy to identify
Beneficial in slow, steady increments

root: languere (Latin) = to be faint or weak
definition: To lose or lack vitality; to suffer from being in an unpleasant situation.
example: The talented athlete languished on the bench all season, waiting for her chance to play.
memory_tip: LANGUID + ANGUISH = LANGUISH — a languid (lazy) anguish, slowly fading away!
wrong_definitions: To grow stronger and thrive in difficult conditions
To burst into activity and renewed energy
To succeed rapidly despite obstacles

definition: Subject to sudden or unpredictable changes of mood; volatile; quicksilver.
example: Her mercurial temperament made it impossible to predict whether she'd be cheerful or furious.
memory_tip: MERCURIAL — MERCURY the planet zips around the sun fastest! Quick-changing and volatile!
wrong_definitions: Steady and completely predictable in mood
Calm and never prone to emotional outbursts
Slow to change and highly consistent

definition: Failing to take proper care in doing something; careless about one's responsibilities.
example: The negligent lifeguard was distracted by his phone when a swimmer got into trouble.
memory_tip: NEGLI-GENT — a GENT who NEGLEcts his duties! Careless and inattentive!
wrong_definitions: Extremely attentive and careful in all duties
Overly cautious to the point of being slow
Thorough and meticulous in every task

definition: Stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion or chosen course of action.
example: The obstinate child refused to eat vegetables no matter how they were prepared.
memory_tip: OB-STINATE — OBSTacles don't stop them; STINATE = staying PUT! Stubbornly fixed!
wrong_definitions: Easily persuaded and quick to change one's mind
Open to all viewpoints and highly flexible
Indecisive and constantly changing position

definition: Complaining in a petulant or whining manner; constantly finding fault.
example: The querulous passenger complained about everything from the temperature to the seat width.
memory_tip: QUER-ULOUS — QUER sounds like QUARREL! Always quarreling and whining!
wrong_definitions: Cheerful and positive even in difficult situations
Grateful and appreciative of whatever is offered
Quiet and content without expressing any complaints

definition: Optimistic or positive, especially in a difficult situation; cheerfully confident.
example: Despite the setbacks, she remained sanguine and convinced the project would succeed.
memory_tip: SANGUINE — SANG + WINE! Singing over wine! Rosy-cheeked and optimistic!
wrong_definitions: Deeply pessimistic and expecting the worst
Anxious and paralyzed by worst-case thinking
Indifferent and emotionally flat
